## Learning outcomes

By the end of this part, you should be able to:

- Direct `Claude Code` to scaffold and extend a `Next.js` application across multiple sessions
- Use `CLAUDE.md`, a `docs/` folder, and the search → paste → cite pattern to keep the agent accurate throughout a build
- Manage feature work through branches and pull requests, with a fresh-session diff review
- Run a `Next.js` app locally and verify the full feature set in your browser

<br>

---

<br>

## Overview

This is the Sprint 1 sprint project. Every habit you have built across this sprint comes together here: directing `Claude Code` with a clear brief, managing context with `CLAUDE.md` and a `docs/` folder, using branches and pull requests, and reviewing diffs with a fresh session.

You will build a lightweight, single-user document management app, a personal browser-based workspace. The app has no backend and no user accounts. That constraint is intentional: a later sprint adds a real database and authentication. For now, the focus is on the framework itself, with one decision you will direct yourself rather than be handed: **how documents are persisted between sessions.** You will work that out with `Claude Code` before you start building features, and you will present the decision and the mechanism you settled on to your reviewer.

**This project runs locally only: it is not deployed anywhere, and you are not expected to put it on the public internet.** You have not been taught how to deploy a `Next.js` app yet, and you do not need to. Everything in this project (building, testing, and submitting) happens on your own machine at `localhost:3000`. Sharing apps on a public URL is something a later sprint covers, once the app has a backend and user accounts to support it.

#### Topics

- `Next.js` App Router: pages, layouts, and dynamic routes
- Choosing a persistence mechanism for a no-backend app, in consultation with `Claude Code`
- Multi-session `Claude Code` workflows and context engineering
- Feature branches, pull requests, and diff review

#### Prerequisites

- Completed the static-site lesson and the version-control lesson in this sprint
- A `GitHub` account
- `Claude Code` installed and authenticated

**Estimated time to complete:** 12 hours

<br>

---

<br>

## Task description

<img src="https://intra-content-image-bucket.s3.eu-north-1.amazonaws.com/images/bc8c3b08-aec0-46f5-8d1b-0466711772fe.png" width="800" alt="Two-pane document workspace layout" />

You are building a personal document management app. A user opens the app and sees a two-pane workspace: a sidebar on the left listing all their documents, and a content area on the right showing the selected document.

The user can create a new document with one click, type a title and body, and see changes saved automatically. Documents persist in the browser across reloads. The sidebar has a search box that filters documents by title as the user types. Each document has its own unique web address, like `/docs/abc123`, so bookmarking or sharing a link takes the user directly back to that document.

The framework stack is fixed:

- `Next.js` (App Router)
- `TypeScript`
- `Tailwind CSS`

**How documents are persisted between sessions is not fixed.** You will decide that yourself after a consultation with `Claude Code` (see *Approach to solving the task* below) and present the decision to your reviewer.

**The app runs locally on your machine via `npm run dev` (the command that starts your app locally). There is no deployment step in this project.** You do not need a hosting account, you do not need to configure environment variables for a hosted environment, and the app does not go on a public URL. Local-only is the whole point: it keeps the focus on directing the agent inside `Next.js`. Deployment is taught in a later sprint, once the app has a real backend.

You will not write any of this code yourself. Your job is to write the brief, keep the agent accurate with context and references, review diffs, and own the outcome.

<img src="https://intra-content-image-bucket.s3.eu-north-1.amazonaws.com/images/e0941581-e447-4651-8bd2-22ad712b3517.png" width="800" alt="Markdown editor with edit and preview modes" />


<br>

---

<br>

## Task requirements

#### The exact task requirements are as follows

1. A home page at `/` with a short description and a link to the workspace.
2. A workspace at `/docs` showing the two-pane layout: sidebar on the left, content area on the right.
3. A sidebar listing all documents sorted by most recently updated, with a search box that filters by title as the user types.
4. A **New document** button in the sidebar that creates a blank document and opens it immediately.
5. An editor for each document (e.g. `/docs/abc123`) with a title field and a body editor. Changes save automatically (no manual save required), using whichever persistence mechanism you settled on after the consultation.
6. Documents persist across a full page reload. Navigating directly to a document's address (e.g. `/docs/abc123`) opens the correct document.
7. Navigating to a document address that does not exist shows a "Document not found" message with a link back to the workspace.
8. Each document in the sidebar has a delete control. Clicking it asks for confirmation before removing the document.
9. Readable empty states throughout: no blank screens when there are no documents or no search results.
10. The body supports basic formatting (headings, bold, italic, bullet lists) using Markdown (a simple text format). A toggle or separate edit/preview mode is required.
11. Pressing Enter while typing the title should jump the cursor straight to the body field; the user shouldn't need to click. A user should be able to create and write a document without touching the mouse.
12. A usable responsive layout: side-by-side at desktop width, stacked or collapsible at phone width.

<img src="https://intra-content-image-bucket.s3.eu-north-1.amazonaws.com/images/060895a3-373d-4724-825e-811bb52104d7.png" width="800" alt="Desktop and mobile responsive layouts of the document app" />


<br>

---

<br>

## Optional tasks

Complete at least one optional task via a dedicated feature branch and pull request.

#### Easy

- **Starred documents.** A star button on each document pins favourites to the top of the sidebar. Starred state persists across reloads alongside the documents.
- **Dark-mode toggle.** A toggle switches the app between light and dark themes, and the choice persists across page reloads. (You don't need to specify how; the agent knows Tailwind's built-in dark-mode support.)
- **Document word count.** Display a live word count beneath the body editor.

#### Medium

- **Tags.** Add a tags field to the document form. Show tags on each sidebar entry. Allow filtering the sidebar by tag.
- **Export / import.** A button to download the full workspace as a `JSON` file; a file picker to re-import from a previously exported file.
- **Document history.** Store the last three saved versions of a document and let the user restore a previous version.

#### Hard

- **Soft delete and trash.** Deleted documents move to a "Trash" section of the sidebar rather than being removed immediately. A **Restore** button returns them. An **Empty trash** action removes them permanently.
- **Keyboard command palette.** A `Cmd+K` / `Ctrl+K` shortcut opens a palette listing all documents and actions. Typing filters the list; `Enter` navigates or triggers the selected action.
- **Folder structure.** Allow documents to be organised into named folders shown as collapsible groups in the sidebar.

<br>

---

<br>

## Evaluation criteria

This project is reviewed locally. There is no deployed URL and no hosted environment to check: open the app at `localhost:3000` to evaluate the criteria below.

1. The app meets all twelve task requirements when run locally with `npm run dev` at `localhost:3000`, including data that persists across reloads and readable empty states throughout.
   Weight: 3

2. The optional task is delivered through a dedicated feature branch and a merged pull request, and the repo has at least two merged PRs in total, with a fresh-session diff review noted on at least one of them. `.gitignore` covers `node_modules/`, `.env`, and OS/editor clutter.
   Weight: 1

3. `CLAUDE.md` at the repo root reflects the rules the agent was actually asked to follow, and a `docs/` folder contains at least one cited `Next.js` reference with a source URL at the top.
   Weight: 2

4. The student dedicated 30 to 60 minutes to a focused design pass, directing `Claude Code` with specific visual direction (typography, spacing, colour, layout density, component shape) rather than a vague "make it nicer". The final result shows deliberate improvement over the scaffolded default, and the prompts and design choices are captured in `REFLECTION.md`.
   Weight: 1

5. The learner can explain the stack end-to-end and justify key design choices, including the persistence decision they reached after consulting `Claude Code`: which mechanism was chosen, why it fits a single-user no-backend app, what alternatives the consultation surfaced, and how prompts described pages and individual document addresses (e.g. `/docs/abc123`) in behaviour-first language rather than naming framework files.
   Weight: 2

<br>

## Creating the web app

<img src="https://intra-content-image-bucket.s3.eu-north-1.amazonaws.com/images/64815b69-cf7e-4df9-8946-ffa869eeea73.png" width="600" alt="Next.js App Router layouts and pages documentation" />

*Next.js App Router layouts and pages documentation*


`Next.js` with the App Router is the chosen framework for this project. It handles routing automatically based on how folders are arranged inside `app/`: each folder maps to a URL on your site. Adding a new page means asking the agent to create a new folder rather than configuring routes by hand. Your `CLAUDE.md` should explicitly tell the agent to use the App Router, not the older Pages Router. You will run the app locally throughout; `npm run dev` starts a local server, and you verify everything in your browser at `localhost:3000`.

You do not need to understand how the App Router works internally to direct the agent effectively.

<br>

---

<br>

## Approach to solving the task

<img src="https://intra-content-image-bucket.s3.eu-north-1.amazonaws.com/images/3c5c812b-011e-4f8c-953b-75191ce463bc.png" width="600" alt="Next.js App Router getting started documentation" />

*Next.js App Router getting started overview with topic navigation*


Start by writing `CLAUDE.md` and seeding a `docs/` folder with at least one cited reference from the `Next.js` documentation before you scaffold the project. The layouts-and-pages reference at `nextjs.org/docs` is a good starting point. Read the relevant sections, paste the content, and add the source URL at the top of the file.

Before you build any features, run a separate consultation with `Claude Code` on persistence. Ask it: given a single-user, browser-based document app with no backend and no user accounts, what is the best way to persist documents between sessions? Paste the constraints in plain language (single user, no backend, no hosted environment, runs on `localhost:3000` only) and let the agent reason about the options. You should walk out of the consultation with a clear answer to two questions: which mechanism you are using, and why it fits this project. Write the choice and the reasoning into `REFLECTION.md`. You will present that decision to your reviewer.

Build one feature at a time. A good sequence is: workspace shell (sidebar + content slot) → CRUD and persistence (using whichever mechanism the consultation landed on) → search and empty states → responsive layout and Markdown → optional task. Commit at every stable state. Do not ask the agent to build the whole app in a single prompt.

Once the twelve task requirements are passing locally, take 30 to 60 minutes for a focused design pass. Brief `Claude Code` with specific direction rather than a vague "make it nicer": pick a tone (clean and minimal, dense and information-rich, playful, editorial), call out typography, spacing, colour palette, button shape, sidebar density, and any UI patterns you want it to follow. Iterate. The final result should look intentional, not like the out-of-the-box scaffolded default. Capture the direction you gave and the choices that landed in `REFLECTION.md` so you can walk your reviewer through them.

If a session goes wrong and the app is in a broken state, reset to the last good commit rather than prompting your way out. Describe this moment in `REFLECTION.md`.

**If you get stuck:** Open a fresh `Claude Code` session, share where you are, and ask it to identify what is most likely blocking you. A fresh session often spots context drift that a long session cannot see.

<br>

## Submission

Read an [in-depth guide about reviews here](https://doku.turingcollege.com/p/0771306d-bc1e-4b00-9153-c63adeb63a85).

#### Submission and scheduling a project review

To submit the project and allow the reviewer to view your work beforehand, use the `GitHub` repository that has been created for you. Please go through these materials to learn more about submitting projects, scheduling project reviews, and using `GitHub`:

- [Completing a Sprint, Submitting a Project and Scheduling Reviews](https://doku.turingcollege.com/p/0771306d-bc1e-4b00-9153-c63adeb63a85)
- [Git and GitHub for Beginners](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=RGOj5yH7evk)

Hand in the following before your 1-1 review call:

- **Public `GitHub` repository URL.**
- **`CLAUDE.md`** at the repo root, reflecting the rules the agent was actually asked to follow.
- **`docs/` folder** with at least one cited `Next.js` reference document. The source URL must appear at the top of the file.
- **At least one merged pull request** with a visible diff and a short description of what changed.
- **`README.md`** covering: what the app does, how to run it locally, a screenshot of the workspace, and which optional task you chose.
- **`REFLECTION.md`** (350–550 words) addressing these six prompts:
  1. The persistence consultation: what you asked `Claude Code`, what mechanism it recommended, what alternatives it surfaced, and why you went with the option you did. This is the decision you will walk your reviewer through.
  2. One prompt where the search → paste → cite pattern changed the outcome: what did you search for, what did you paste, and what would the agent have done without it?
  3. One moment where `CLAUDE.md` caught the agent drifting: what did it attempt, and how did `CLAUDE.md` correct it?
  4. The design pass: what specific visual direction did you give `Claude Code` (tone, typography, spacing, colour, components), what changed from the scaffolded default, and which iteration finally felt right?
  5. One thing that was harder than expected compared to the plain-`HTML` app from the static-site lesson.
  6. What you would keep or change in your `docs/` folder next time: what was useful, what was noise?
